title: The Victory of Faith
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Victory of Faith - Pastor Tito Lyro - 1 John 5_1-5.txt
outline: Introduction: 1 John 5:1-5 — two-part sermon series on this passage
Believing Jesus is the Christ as the mark of new birth
Love for God and love for His children as connected
Keeping God's commandments as the expression of love
Faith as the victory that overcomes the world
Who overcomes the world? He who believes Jesus is the Son of God
Conclusion: The present and future victory of faith
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches the first of two sermons on 1 John 5:1-5, exploring the connection between faith, love, and victory. He shows that believing Jesus is the Christ marks the new birth, and that love for God and His children manifests in keeping His commandments — a faith that overcomes the world.
transcript:
The famous French economist Frédéric Bastiat said, "Repetition may not entertain, but it teaches." John the apostle wholeheartedly subscribed to this truth. He keeps repeating the necessity of faith, love, and obedience in the Christian life.

This repetition should teach us at least two things. First, faith, love, and obedience are very important. In Scripture and in life, repetition often signals importance. The fact that John keeps repeating this idea should tell us that the Holy Spirit wants us to pay attention to it. Second, we need to hear it over and over again because we are prone to wander from these three core tenets of the Christian life: faith in Jesus, love for the brethren, obedience to the Word of God.

In the five verses we are studying today, we have a microcosm of the whole epistle. One of our members came to me at the beginning of this series and said, "It seems like every sermon is the same in this book." And yes, in some ways it is, because John keeps on repeating himself. And in these five verses, you have the whole book repeated again.

## The Structure of 1 John 5:1-5

John keeps hitting the key of three tests of true faith that lead to assurance:

- **Right doctrine about Christ** — Verse 1 says, "Jesus is the Christ," the Messiah; verse 5 says, "Jesus is the Son of God," with everything that entails. Do you believe the right things about Christ?
- **Love for the brethren** — Verse 1 (second half) raises this test. Do you love the brethren?
- **Obedience to the Word of God** — Verse 3: the commandments of the Lord are not burdensome. Do you obey the Lord?

And in verses 4 and 5, John recasts the idea of assurance in terms of overcoming the world, which we will look at next week.

Notice how many words are repeated in just these five verses. The words *believe*, *faith*, and related terms appear again and again. The word *born* or *begotten* recurs. The word *love* is repeated. The word *commandments* is repeated. The words *overcome* and *victory* — the same root in the original language — are repeated. Repetition indicates emphasis.

Also notice that the passage is framed by faith in Jesus Christ: verse 1 and verse 5 are both about faith in the Lord Jesus Christ. These three characteristics — love for the brethren, faith in Jesus Christ, obedience to the Word of God — are deeply intertwined. Have you ever mixed Play-Doh colors? At first they are beautifully separate. A day or two later they are thoroughly blended together — you can still identify hints of each color here and there, but you cannot really separate them anymore. That is the idea here. Faith in Christ, love for the brethren, and obedience to the Word of God are so intertwined you can only separate them to study them.

## Faith in Jesus Christ

Faith in Jesus Christ, the Christ of the Bible, is the foundational characteristic of the Christian. A Christian is a Christian because he believes certain things about Jesus Christ.

In verse 1, John says that a Christian is a Christian because he believes that Jesus is the anointed one from God — that is what "Christ" means. He came in fulfillment of all the prophecies of the Old Testament to redeem God's elect from bondage to sin. That is all contained in the phrase "Jesus is the Christ."

The Christian also believes that Jesus is the unique Son of God, which means He is God Himself come in the flesh — that is what John means in verse 5.

However, faith in Christ involves more than merely assenting to the proposition that Jesus is the promised Messiah. The Christian faith is a fact-driven faith, but mere knowledge of certain facts is not the totality of faith. James addresses this when he says sarcastically, "You believe in God? Great! So do the demons." Subscribing to a biblically accurate confession of faith is not all there is to faith. Faith is not less than that — but there is more.

### The Three Elements of Faith

Theologians describe saving faith by dividing it into three elements:

**Knowledge** — You must know the gospel. Paul says in Romans 10, quoting Isaiah, "Faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of God." You need to know that Jesus is the Christ, that He is the Son of God.

**Assent** — This is the intellectual or cognitive conviction that the knowledge you have acquired about Christ is indeed factually true, and that the provisions of the gospel correspond exactly to your actual needs. Not just knowing things about Christ, but acknowledging that those things are true and that what Christ is offering is what you need.

But that is still not all. I know the fact that I am out of shape. I acknowledge that as true. I know that I need to lose weight, and I know there are really only two ways to do it. I know the facts, and I know I need it. Has it worked for me yet? Just knowing something to be true, and knowing that you need it, does not mean you have appropriated it. That leads to the third element.

**Trust** — Assent is cognition passing into conviction; trust is conviction passing into confidence. It is embracing Christ: "You are what I need. You are who you say you are. You have done what you said you have done, for me. I embrace it as mine." That is the third element of faith. Paul says in 2 Timothy 1:12: *"I know whom I have believed and am persuaded that He is able to keep what I have committed to Him until that Day."* The Greek word for "persuaded" there is actually the word for belief with a different ending — to be persuaded is to be convinced that certain beliefs are true and to act on them.

To those who possess this kind of faith, the Father has given them the right to become His children. John 1:12-13: *"But as many as received Him, to them He gave the right to become children of God, to those who believe in His name: who were born, not of blood, nor of the will of the flesh, nor of the will of man, but of God."*

### Faith Is the Result of God's Work

Notice in verse 1 that saving faith is always the result of God's gracious work in a person's heart. The ESV translates it clearly: "Everyone who believes presently that Jesus is the Christ has been born of God in the past." The being born of God is something that happened in the past and continues to bear fruit now. True faith in Jesus Christ is the result of regeneration — God regenerates the person and, as a result, that person believes.

The Scriptures also speak of this new birth in terms of a change of heart — a core promise of the new covenant. Ezekiel 36 says that God is going to give us a new heart, and because of that new heart, we are able to believe, to follow Him, and to do the things the Spirit reveals to us. The Bible also speaks of this new birth as a spiritual resurrection. Ephesians 2:4-5:

> *"But God, who is rich in mercy, because of His great love with which He loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ (by grace you have been saved)."*

Faith that saves follows from a heart that has been changed, a heart that has been born again, a heart resurrected from being dead in trespasses and sins and now made alive in Jesus Christ.

## Love for the Brethren

Notice that the new birth will inevitably result in love for others who have also been born of God. Verse 1: *"Everyone who loves Him who begot also loves him who is begotten of Him."* In John's mind, if you have been born of God, you cannot help but love others who have also been born of God. It is part of the new spiritual DNA.

Think of the example of Paul. He was commissioned by the leaders in Jerusalem to go arrest and harm Christians. Luke describes him as breathing threats and murder against the disciples of the Lord. He had other plans for them beyond just arresting them. Then Jesus meets him on the road to Damascus, changes his heart — and the threats and murders are immediately replaced with fellowship with the people of God. Acts 9:19 ends the account of Paul's conversion with this: "Then Saul spent some days with the disciples at Damascus." He dwelt with them. Immediately upon conversion, Paul loved the brethren. That is true of all of us who have been changed by the Spirit of God.

### Love Through Obedience

We love the brethren by loving and obeying God. Verses 2-3:

> *"By this we know that we love the children of God, when we love God and keep His commandments. For this is the love of God, that we keep His commandments. And His commandments are not burdensome."*

See how we are going from faith to love to obedience — you cannot really separate them. One of the ways we love the brethren is by obeying God. Genuine love for God will show itself in love for other Christians. It is also impossible to obey God apart from loving the brethren. You can cross every T and dot every I in the Bible, but if you do not love the brethren, you are not obeying God. As Paul says in 1 Corinthians 13:1: *"Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels, but have not love, I have become sounding brass or a clanging cymbal."*

### What Love Is

At this point it is important to define love, because we are very good at disguising not-love as love.

Three things John says about love in 1 John:

**First**, love is others-oriented. 1 John 4:10: *"In this is love, not that we loved God, but that He loved us and sent His Son to be the propitiation for our sins."* If your definition of love has you at the center, that is not love.

**Second**, love costs something to the one loving. 1 John 4:9: *"In this the love of God was manifested toward us, that God has sent His only begotten Son into the world, that we might live through Him."* If your definition of love has you always gaining something, that is not love.

**Third**, love is not a response to what the other person has done for you. 1 John 4:19: *"We love because He first loved us."* That is the pattern for our love. If your definition of love demands that someone do something for you first, that is not love.

Love and holiness go hand in hand. John says we know we love the children of God by keeping His commandments. If you say you love the brethren, you are also saying that you are striving to live holy lives in accordance with the Scriptures.

In the Reformed church — perhaps out of a fear of legalism — we have deemphasized the importance of holiness and obedience. Yet the grace that is from God will demonstrate itself in holy lives, not just positionally but also in practice. We are so afraid of adding something to our salvation that we have thrown the baby out with the bathwater. Titus 2:11-14:

> *"For the grace of God that brings salvation has appeared to all men, teaching us that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, we should live soberly, righteously, and godly in the present age, looking for the blessed hope and glorious appearing of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ, who gave Himself for us, that He might redeem us from every lawless deed and purify for Himself His own special people, zealous for good works."*

Grace that is not linked to obedience is not the grace of the Bible. If we are not zealous for good works, we have not experienced the grace of God.

## God's Commandments Are Not Burdensome

Verse 3 says, *"His commandments are not burdensome."* Does this surprise you? It surprises me. The commands of God often feel burdensome. Why?

God's commandments become burdensome in several situations:

- When, like Eve, our eyes are taken off the goodness of God and turned toward the one thing we are denied. Satan came to Eve and said, "God told you that you can't have of this one tree?" Her focus shifted from the goodness of God in all things to this one restriction, and the word became heavy.
- When extra-biblical requirements are elevated to the level of biblical necessities for our justification. The Pharisees went out of their way to add heavy burdens to people's shoulders.
- When we have a divided heart that longs for the world more than for the kingdom of God. When we have one eye in the world and one eye on Christ, the commands of God become a burden.
- When we are living in unrepentant sin. Proverbs 15 says the way of the unfaithful is hard. Proverbs 28 says he who covers his sin will not prosper.
- When we have been misled by false teachers. In Acts 20:29, Paul warns the Ephesian elders — the very elders of the church John is writing to — that after his departure, savage wolves will burden the church with things not from Scripture. The Greek word Paul uses there for "savage" is the same word translated "burdensome" in 1 John.
- When we view obedience as a means to earn acceptance before God. If you think God's love for you is based on your daily performance, then every day you fall short — which is every day — becomes a crushing burden. But brothers and sisters, Christ has done it. He said, "It is finished." We are accepted in the Beloved. He has translated us from the kingdom of darkness into the kingdom of the Son He loves. We are so united with Christ that when the Father looks at you, He sees the face of His Son whom He loves eternally.
- When we are not saved. The natural man cannot please God; the commands of God are a burden to him.

Brothers and sisters, what God asks us to do is not a heavy burden. Jesus says: *"Come to Me, all you who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest, for My yoke is easy and My burden is light."* We have been completely recreated in order to do good works. Ephesians 2:10: *"For we are His workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand that we should walk in them."* We cannot but obey the will of God. The Spirit of God, as John says in chapter 4 verse 4, is in us and able to obey.

Christian obedience is supernatural, but it is not superhuman — and there is a difference. The Apostle tells us in 1 Corinthians 10:13 that nothing has overtaken us except what is common to man. Our struggles are not superhuman; we may need the supernatural help of the Spirit to stand, but the struggles themselves are not superhuman.

Instead of thinking of obedience as a burden, let us think of it as a blessing.

## Three Practical Applications

**First**, if you are really struggling to love others and to obey the Lord, check your faith to see if it is real. Don't be deceived. 2 Corinthians 13:5 says: *"Examine yourselves as to whether you are in the faith."*

**Second**, if you are struggling with the idea that God's commandments are not burdensome, read through Psalm 119 tonight. It is 176 verses, but it only takes about twenty minutes. The psalmist again and again tells us how precious God's commandments are, contrasting obedience to the Word with following the ways of the world. Let the psalmist and the Spirit talk to you about the preciousness of the commands of the Lord.

**Third**, since you have been recreated in Jesus Christ, loving the brethren is what God designed you to do. Think of concrete ways you are going to do this this week. Two exercises to help: search "one another" in the New Testament on BibleGateway.com — all those results are ways you can love the brethren this week. And look at questions 128 through 148 in the Westminster Larger Catechism, which explain and apply the Ten Commandments — you will come up with all kinds of practical ideas for how to love the brethren, since loving the brethren is obeying the law.

We have been born of God, and because of that we believe in the Lord Jesus Christ. That faith impacts this life as we obey the Word of God and love the brethren. Three elements of being a Christian: believe in Jesus, love the brethren, obey God's Word.
